author: @BuildingPersephone
text:
You'll need to redirect the flow of water upstream to take it off the internal walls. Otherwise, I imagine in a real-world rainwter scenario, the vast majority of the water will adhere to the internal surfaces of the downspout by action of surface tension and then skate past the turbine. I would think (but you'd have to test this to be sure) that at any given point in the pipe, the concentration of water in that crossesion is actually lowest in the centre. I would add a section of water conditioning immediately above the turbine with the aim of concentrating and redirecting the flow of water as close as possible to a laminar flow without slowing the water so much it backs up in the downspout. 

Actually, come to think about it, a better idea might be to add a small reservoir, say 600mm of downspout, above the laminar collector, topped off with a steeply angled mesh to filter out leaves and debris, leading immediately to an angled overflow spout. The main downpipe then drains into a waterbutt, while the angled overflow leads to a ground drain. The ideal situation then is that the flow is not interrupted enough to fill the 600mm reservoir, thus capturing all of the rainwater's potential energy, and the water butt is utlised frequently enough that it never fills.

author: @keXen
text:
A really nice build Robert :) I love how you fit stuff into ordinary bits and parts. 

About rainwater power; Maybe my math is incorrect, but the truth of the story is that it's "nothing" there to harvest right?

Let's say your roof are 150 square meters, and the annual amount of rain in UK is 1100 mm. That gives 165.000 liters of water "landing" on your roof.
If your rain gutter is 3 meter long, the energy in the water is 4.855.950 J witch converts to 1,39 kWh, without any losses to the environment or in the conversion.

In reality I would guesstimate that only 50% of the energy is harvestable, and we are left with 0.7 kWh production each year.

Are my math/physics way of here?

author: @gavinlynas2833
text:
I would imagine the water going down the pipe would favour running down the sides causing it to miss most of the desired area. I think a funnel shape just above the spike would force the water go though from the centre outwards. This may cause the water flow to bottleneck though and overflowing in the gutters. I'd imagine stacking them might also cause bottlenecking. 

The other issue would be preventing debris from clogging things up over time. Even very tiny bits would cause an issue. There would have to be a fine mesh but that mesh could soon clog up.

author: @steve_jabz
text: I've always wondered what would happen if you had arrays of giant pipes stacked with generators in them all the way down. Surely it would continue to push the next wheel and the next and the next, right? It's not as if it can defy gravity and stop falling, but energy also has to be lost (transferred) from another system to maintain conservation of energy, so what is that like, a minuscule amount of the energy from the gravitational field of earth?

Suggestion: for just a wee bit more power harvesting, one could put some counter diverted vanes in the top of the spout [they need not be very specially complex or sophisticated, and you could put them in without printing, just take a top of a matching steel soup can, cut it into 'petals', turn them so that they look like fan blades, and then hot glue it into the top so that it gives the falling water just a little spin so that it is turning into the thrusting face of the turbine blades.

While at the amount of water flow normal rainfall may not really make the system spin, this will give you [[[as much as]]] 7% more effort from the water as it falls in, by giving it that directional spin.

replies.author: @keXen
replies.text:
Sunlight delivers around 1.2 kW pr m2, or 1.2 kWh in one hour.

If your house is 6 meters high, you need 75.000 liters of water on your roof to "store" 1.2 kWh of energy.

I know there are more rain then sun in the UK, but I hope you don't get 75.000 liters of rain for each hour of sunshine :p

replies.author: @simonwatson5299
replies.text: @keXen I think you're maths is a*se over t*t. Rob just made a generator for rainwater downpipes for literally £40. You could buy a bunch of these, and generate enough power much quicker in the UK, than if you spent tons of money on very expensive panels, which will generate power, but because the UK is in the Northern Hemisphere, we get less sunlight per year than other countries, (not including scandinavian countries and others that are further North) and as we're an Island, our weather is often overcast/ and or otherwise raining. We do get good sunshine, but not consistantly. Windpower and rainwater gennies are the way to go over here. (Bloody hell I talk a load of old b*llox).

author: @ChristieNel
text: That is a beautiful piece of kit.  I would design it to assume a much lower flow - probably a much smaller turbine.  If it could somehow maintain a certain water height in the pipe above the turbine, the pressure should make it much more efficient.

replies.author: @vmonk722
replies.text:
You could add a reservoir with a bell syphon between the input and the turbine. That would result in a defined pressure, flow rate and minimum flow duration for the system, allowing for some further optimization of the turbine and the electrical components. Furthermore, it would allow harvesting energy even from a slight trickle of rain that could slowly accumulate in the reservoir until the syphon activates and flushes the reservoir.
This might not necessarily be a good idea however as you pay for those benefits with a potential for frost damage or, even if the system doesn't break when frozen, a good chance that it might get clogged by ice during the cold months. I suppose painting the reservoir black and surrounding it with one or two transparent shells for isolation could at least lead to a system that uncloggs itself during the day if it froze during the night.

replies.author: @ChristieNel
replies.text: @vmonk722 That's a good idea, actually.  And frost is definitely an issue to consider.  An easy solution would be to allow a small leak, which won't massively affect efficiency while raining, but will eventually drain.

author: @pedjamilosavljevic6235
text: This is a really good prototype for a product actually. Besides the obvious improvements (more wire and stronger magnets) , the inner cone (fondle) on the top of the assembly  should direct water, maybe, at a slight incline towards desired rotor direction of rotation (some "ribs" - straight , or slightly curved). Similar approach like with Darwin's wt - manipulating the available fluid flow. Other people already mentioned some kind of protection against the debris in the drain pipe and I would add - an easement of installation/de-instillation for freezing conditions and cleaning  (maybe a rubber sleeve on top ). Otherwise brilliant stuff !

author: @MrBlueEyez
text: I can just image future homes having multiples of these in each downspout, with a joule thief integrated, and all of them wired together to charge a battery bank someplace in the house or garage.

author: @HuSiaCat
text:
Towers that drop water inside them create downward air force at great speed, which drives turbines at the bottom. Overcoming the water pressure and energy required to raise the water is apparently a relatively simple process. This is the most effective way of generating clean energy, the water of course can be recycled and used over and over, factoring in evaporation. 

We won't ever see them, or will we...??

 Would love to see a smaller working model Robert. If it takes your fancy ?!

author: @61joro

author: @gordonlawrence1448
text: I cant help wondering what sort of current you get out of this. Specifically if you use a schottky diode bridge and a joule thief to bump up the voltage would you need to use something a bit more heavy duty than a small signal transistor/? You could also then use a 34063 type buck converter to get a nice steady 5V for charging a phone etc. If the voltage is high enough you would not need the joule thief stage. Also using wire that is too thin for the coil will obviously restrict current. So basically you could use the flat rainwater equivalent of a PV on the roof then this in the downsprout. The maximum you are going to get from the turbine for a standard house is less than you would think. IE 100mm a month is the average. That is 100kg of water per square meter. Even the Highlands only get about three times that. It's less in the south east. From roof to ground is 4.7m call it 5 for easy maths. So that is a mere 5000J (taking G to be 10 for easy maths). Assuming the whole lot comes down in 5 hours (unlikely) for a 100% efficient power output you are talking 0.05W per square meter of footprint (not planar area) of roof feeding that downsprout so maybe 0,5W max. That said I am working on turning the electrostatic rainfall generator using microscope slides. That looks like it should achieve far more power.

author: @mikedodger7898
text:
What about this crazy idea: this is an insert to your rain water pipe, and the mechanism is inside the enclosure ... but, it is only useful when it is raining.

If you were to orient this vertically (like a water wheel) to have both an inside (water) AND outside (wind) powered rotation, you could get more use out of the motor system. Plus, the generator and wiring could be housed in the "air" side so there are less chances of shorting the wires. 

Crazy?

author: @kevintipcorn6787
text: Trying to think of a way to counter debris and dirt. it'd be amazing to fit small ones in sinks and shower/bath drains. All that energy literally going town the drain.

author: @mikedodger7898
text: To build on Jehu's note, water wheels have the water falling on one side of the wheel. Thus, would this perform better if the water was more channeled and vertical?

replies.text: This one turns 100% of the area into the rotation, that makes is completely space efficient. So I dont see any improvements with this turbine. Maybe other designs are better optimized for other parameters but always and up larger and heavier.

author: @David_Mash
text:
Is this better than a Pelton wheels setup? 
Often the stream is thinned down to a jet for a Pelton wheels, but I don't think that would be necessary with this turbine style if all volume will be utilized

replies.author: @kaboom-zf2bl
replies.text: if yoou get the input or top of the pipe to be a vortex cone then near the bottom the generator will be able to take the full flow the pipe sucks down it and no pelton needed ... one could put 4 or 5 of these just before the outflow bend then 3 or 4 generators then pipe upto a vortex cone entrance fed from rainwater ... then poof you max out your power potential in a short period ... hook it into a step up transformer setup and you can get useful power and current out of it too

replies.author: @David_Mash
replies.text: @0623kaboom  Pelton wheels are often used for net zero housing with stream access but this is an interesting design because it seems high volume and low head could be fine.

replies.author: @kaboom-zf2bl
replies.text: @David_Mash yes ... the vortex has a nice added bonus in that it will actually suck a larger volume of water through the system making for better results if one can keep ahead of it's pull ... this is where victor schaubergers vortex ideas come in to play perfectly ...

replies.author: @David_Mash
replies.text: ​@kaboom-zf2bl I just mentioned Viktor in 1884! So much missed opportunity there

author: @peterkent2138
text: Once again, outstanding design.

author: @JehuMcSpooran
text: Nice one. Though I do wonder how much energy is lost from the water having to change direction on the cone. Otherwise, nice design. Neat and compact. It could be used with any fluid, even sand.

replies.author: @mikedodger7898
replies.text: I was wondering something similar. Water wheels have the water falling on one side of the wheel.

replies.author: @kaboom-zf2bl
replies.text: here's the fun part NONE... because the cone is a converging nozzle it actually speeds up the water making it able to do more work for the same volume .... then the water hits a diverging cone where it slows down pushing harder on the turbine making it generate better ... all the while the water will start to swirl allowing for an even higher flow rate of water ... if one has a long enough pipe that forces the water into a double helix shape then you will approach nearly twice the flow rate of the pipe as compared to just dropping down ... so if the pipe can do 5 gallons a minute ... with the double helix flow it will be closer to 10gallons a minute ... ... so that 5v reading would be 10 to 20v ... depends on the windings ... if it was a good x4 step up winding set then near 20v ...

replies.author: @mikedodger7898
replies.text: Is there a limit to the number of these that could be stacked? This is gravity fed, so the only limit is the height?

replies.author: @kaboom-zf2bl
replies.text: @mikedodger7898 how long is your drain spout ...

replies.author: @kaboom-zf2bl
replies.text: @mikedodger7898 the only real limit is how much flow the pipe gets ... so a section of pipe with a  victor schauberger twist in it ... look that one up it's relevant ... then the average drain pipe of 10 feet could easily support 4 or 5 of the generators with a 5 foot drop and each gen abut a foot long ..
